coredata.dc:title: Green synthesis of pH-sensitive magnetic bio-nanocomposite hydrogel based on galactomannan and sodium alginate for targeted colorectal cancer drug delivery
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Science: Advanced Materials and Devices
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Vietnam National University, Hanoi. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: Vietnam National University, Hanoi. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Tahmasebi, Shabnam
Mohammadi, Reza
coredata.dc:description: This study presents the development and characterization of pH-sensitive bio-nanocomposite hydrogel beads designed for the targeted delivery of doxorubicin (DOX) in colorectal cancer (CRC) therapy. The hydrogels were synthesized using galactomannan (GM) and sodium alginate (SA) matrices, reinforced with Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs) to enhance drug release control and therapeutic efficacy. The MNPs were functionalized through a green synthesis approach, utilizing copper oxide nanoparticles (CuO NPs) reduced and stabilized by orange peel extract (OPE) and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) derived from tea leaves. Characterization techniques (FTIR, XRD, SEM, VSM, PL) confirmed the successful synthesis of the carrier components. In vitro drug release studies demonstrated pronounced pH-sensitive behavior, with minimal release (<14 %) observed at pH 1.2 and substantial release (>88 %) at pH 7.4, indicating potential for targeted delivery to the colorectal region. The carrier exhibited significant antioxidant and antibacterial activity effects against Gram-negative Escherichia coli (>94 % inhibition) and Gram-positive Staphylococcus aureus (>99 % inhibition). Moreover, MTT assays revealed significant cytotoxicity effects of drug-loaded carriers against HT-29 CRC cells. These findings showed the promising potential of synthesized materials for targeted CRC therapy, offering a synergistic combination of controlled drug release, antioxidant properties, and antibacterial activity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Drug delivery
Colorectal cancer
Bio-nanocomposite hydrogels
Carbon quantum dot
Magnetic nanoparticles

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and functionalization of Fe3O4 MNPs
Kinetic studies of in-vitro drug release
Swelling and DOX release behavior studies

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2025 Vietnam National University, Hanoi. Published by Elsevier B.V.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) FTIR spectra of Fe3O4, GM, SA, OPE, CuO, CQD, GM-SA/Fe3O4@CuO@CQD, GM-SA/Fe3O4@CuO@CQD-DOX. b) XRD patterns of Fe3O4, GM, SA, OPE, CuO, CQD, GM-SA/Fe3O4@CuO@CQD.
SEM images of (a) Fe3O4, (b) CQD, (c) CuO NPs, (d) GM-SA/Fe3O4@CuO@CQD and (f) GM-SA/Fe3O4@CuO@CQD -DOX, EDX/MAP of (e) GM-SA/Fe3O4@CuO@CQD, (g) GM-SA/Fe3O4@CuO@CQD -DOX.

a) Magnetic hysteresis loops for Fe3O4 MNPs and GM-SA/Fe3O4@CuO@CQD. b) UV–vis absorption CQD solution shown under light irradiation, wavelength-dependent fluorescent emission spectra of CQD and GM-SA/Fe3O4@CuO@CQD
a) Swelling behavior of GM-SA/Fe3O4@CuO@CQD in pH 1.2, 6.8, 7.4 (n = 3). b) Drug release behavior of GM-SA/Fe3O4@CuO@CQD in pH 1.2, 6.8, 7.4 (n = 3).

a) Antibacterial activity colonies of E. coli and S. aureus on agar plates for control, OPE, CuO NPs, GM-SA/Fe3O4@CuO@CQD. b) Concentration-dependent percentage inhibition of DPPH Radical and IC50 (2.1 mg/mL) by GM/SA/Fe3O4@CuO@CQD (n = 3).
In vitro cell viability of HT-29 cell line after a 48 h incubation with different concentrations of GM-SA/Fe3O4@CuO@CQD and GM-SA/Fe3O4@CuO@CQD-DOX and free DOX solution.
a) Synthesis of CuO NPs from OPE. b) Extraction of GM from Fenugreek seeds. c) Synthesis of GM-SA/Fe3O4@CuO@CQD

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Quantity of released drug at time t = F; Release exponent = n and b; Scale parameter = a; Constants rate of release = K0, K1, Khc, Kp, Kkp and Kh.

head.ce:title: Green synthesis of pH-sensitive magnetic bio-nanocomposite hydrogel based on galactomannan and sodium alginate for targeted colorectal cancer drug delivery

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Polymer Research Laboratory, Department of Organic and Biochemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, Tabriz, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Polymer Research Laboratory, Department of Organic and Biochemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, Tabriz, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study presents the development and characterization of pH-sensitive bio-nanocomposite hydrogel beads designed for the targeted delivery of doxorubicin (DOX) in colorectal cancer (CRC) therapy. The hydrogels were synthesized using galactomannan (GM) and sodium alginate (SA) matrices, reinforced with Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs) to enhance drug release control and therapeutic efficacy. The MNPs were functionalized through a green synthesis approach, utilizing copper oxide nanoparticles (CuO NPs) reduced and stabilized by orange peel extract (OPE) and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) derived from tea leaves. Characterization techniques (FTIR, XRD, SEM, VSM, PL) confirmed the successful synthesis of the carrier components. In vitro drug release studies demonstrated pronounced pH-sensitive behavior, with minimal release (<14 %) observed at pH 1.2 and substantial release (>88 %) at pH 7.4, indicating potential for targeted delivery to the colorectal region. The carrier exhibited significant antioxidant and antibacterial activity effects against Gram-negative Escherichia coli (>94 % inhibition) and Gram-positive Staphylococcus aureus (>99 % inhibition). Moreover, MTT assays revealed significant cytotoxicity effects of drug-loaded carriers against HT-29 CRC cells. These findings showed the promising potential of synthesized materials for targeted CRC therapy, offering a synergistic combination of controlled drug release, antioxidant properties, and antibacterial activity.

ce:section.ce:para: Colorectal cancer (CRC) is one of the leading causes of cancer-related deaths worldwide [1]. To cure CRC, numerous treatments such as chemotherapy, hormone therapy, surgery, etc. have been used. However, some limitations often make these types of treatment ineffective. For instance, chemotherapy has serious side effects such as reducing the negative impacts of drugs on healthy tissues, accurately directing medications to tumor tissues, managing the release of drugs in the body, and additional associated challenges [2–5]. Therefore, developing smart drug delivery systems (DDSs) that can precisely target the colorectal region is needed [6]. Recent advancements in DDSs, particularly nanostructured carriers, have shown significant promise in enhancing drug bioavailability, minimizing side effects, and providing controlled release profiles for antibiotic and anticancer therapies [7,8]. pH-sensitive hydrogel-based materials are a type of smart DDSs that respond to changes in pH, making them highly suitable for use in the gastrointestinal tract. These pH-responsive hydrogels undergo a swelling process in response to alterations in the environmental pH, subsequently promoting the release of encapsulated therapeutic agents within specific tissues (cancerous tissues). This characteristic allows for the precise spatial and temporal control of drug release, potentially enhancing therapeutic efficacy while minimizing the side effects [9–14]. Recent developments in stimuli-responsive systems, including pH-sensitive hydrogels and magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs), have demonstrated enhanced therapeutic efficacy and safety by combining natural polymers [15,16]. Natural polymers, such as sodium alginate (SA) and galactomannan (GM), are ideal for preparing these pH-sensitive hydrogels due to their biocompatibility, non-toxicity, biodegradability, and stability in the varying pH environments of the stomach and small intestine [17–23].

However, polysaccharide-based hydrogels may exhibit mechanical weaknesses, which can limit their performance in drug delivery [24–26]. The incorporation of Fe3O4 MNPs into hydrogels not only enhances their mechanical properties and functionalities but also strengthens their magnetic properties, thereby improving drug release control [27–33]. Nevertheless, the dispersion of pure Fe3O4 MNPs can result in deposition on the gastrointestinal tract surface, creating challenges for their removal after administration [34]. Modifying Fe3O4 MNPs with functional groups like CuO NPs, and CQDs can enhance their stability and functionality within the digestive system [35,36]. Additionally, the antibacterial, antioxidant, and anticancer properties of CuO NPs and CQDs enhanced the therapeutic potential of hydrogels [37–39]. Also, orange peel extract (OPE) used in synthesizing CuO enhances antibacterial, antitumor, and antimicrobial properties [40,41].

This study aims to develop a pH-sensitive smart bio-nanocomposite hydrogel for targeted DDSs in the treatment of CRC, focusing on improving the efficacy and reducing side effects of conventional therapies. This hydrogel incorporates GM and SA modified with Fe3O4 MNPs, CuO NPs, and CQDs, improving the controlled and effective delivery of drugs in response to pH conditions. Fe3O4 MNPs are functionalized using CQDs derived from tea leaves, and CuO NPs are reduced and established with OPE. The unique characteristics of the synthesized carrier, including pH responsiveness, enhanced drug loading capacity, and targeted drug release, position the engineered carrier as a promising candidate for advanced DDSs.

Fenugreek seeds, orange peel, and tea leaves were obtained from the local supplier in Tabriz, Iran. Doxorubicin hydrochloride was purchased from Sobhan Pharmaceutical Company (Iran). Sodium alginate (viscosity = ≥ 2000 Cp, M/G ratio = 65/35, Mw = 215 kDa) was purchased from Asia Research Company (Iran). FeSO4·7H2O, FeCl3·6H2O, NaOH, and Cu (NO3)2 were purchased from Merck.

The materials were characterized by using different techniques such as Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, X-ray diffraction (XRD), Photoluminescence (PL), and scanning electron microscopy (SEM). Detailed information on the characterization methods is provided in the Supporting Information (Supplementary S1).

The maceration method was employed for the extraction of OPE [42] (Supplementary S2). CuO NPs were synthesized by combining 4 g of OPE with 2 g of copper nitrate (Cu (NO3)2) in 10 mL of distilled water, and heating the mixture at 60 °C for 2 h. The resulting NPs were separated by centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 10 min, washed with distilled water, and dried in an oven at 60 °C. The dried CuO NPs were stored for subsequent use in carrier synthesis. The CuO NPs synthesizing process was illustrated in Scheme 1 a [42].

Tea leaves were washed, dried, and heated in an oven at 200 °C for 2 h. The leaves were powdered and heated at 200 °C for 5 h for carbonization. The cooled powder was dispersed in 10 mL of 0.1 M acetic acid and reacted for 48 h. The dispersion was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 30 min by using a high-speed centrifuge. The supernatant containing tea-derived CQDs was collected and freeze-dried [43].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and functionalization of Fe3O4 MNPs
ce:section.ce:para: Fe3O4 MNPs were synthesized via a chemical coprecipitation method in an aqueous medium. First, 1.38 g of FeSO4·7H2O and 2.7 g of FeCl3·6H2O, were added to 55 mL of aqueous solution then sodium hydroxide (3 M) was added slowly to the solution while stirred vigorously to adjust the pH to approximately 11. The mixture was heated to 80 °C under a nitrogen atmosphere for 2 h. The black precipitate was separated from the solution using an external magnet and washed several times with distilled water to remove any residual reagents [44]. To functionalize the Fe3O4 MNPs, 0.02 g of NPs were dispersed in 2 mL of distilled water at room temperature for 30 min. Next, 0.02 g of CuO was added, and the mixture was stirred at 40 °C for 30 min. Subsequently, 0.02 g of CQDs was introduced, and the reaction was allowed to continue for an additional 45 min. The resulting solution was used for the subsequent hydrogel synthesis steps.

GM was extracted from fenugreek seeds by using the method outlined in Scheme 1b [45]. Further details of the GM extraction procedure are provided in the Supporting Information (Supplementary S3). To prepare the hydrogel, 0.3 g of SA and 0.3 g of GM were dissolved in 25 mL of distilled water at 45 °C under a constant magnetic stirrer. Functionalized Fe3O4 MNPs were added to the mixture and stirred at 35 °C for 1 h. The resulting solution was cooled to ambient temperature and slowly extruded into a 100 mL CaCl2 solution (4 wt %) from a syringe positioned 15 cm above the solution surface. The beads were stirred for 30 min to ensure complete gelation and solidification. Finally, GM-SA/Fe3O4@CuO@CQD beads were washed with distilled water and dried (Scheme 1c).

The swelling behavior of the carrier was evaluated by immersing 10 mg of the dried carrier in 20 mL of buffer solutions at different pH values (1.2, 6.8, and 7.4) at room temperature for 72 h. The swollen carrier was periodically removed from the solution, blotted with filter paper to remove excess liquid, and weighed. Each test was conducted in triplicate (n = 3). The swelling ratio (SR) was calculated using Eq. (1):
(1)
Swelling
ratio
( % )
=
S 1
−
S 2
S 2
× 100
where S1 is the weight of the swollen carrier and S2 is the weight of the dry carrier [46].

The DOX loading and encapsulation efficiency of the magnetic carrier were determined by adding 100 mg of the carrier with 10 mL of a 100 ppm (mg/L) DOX solution in phosphate buffer (pH 7.4). The mixture was agitated on a shaker at room temperature in a dark place for 48 h. Afterward, the DOX-loaded carrier was separated using a magnet, rinsed with deionized water to remove unbound DOX, and dried at room temperature in a dark place. DOX concentration in the remaining solution was measured by using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer at λ max = 483 nm. All experiments were performed in triplicate (n = 3). Drug loading capacity and encapsulation efficiency were calculated using Eqs. (2) and (3) [47,48].
(2)
Loading
Capacity
(
LC
%
)
=
weight
of
drug
in
beads
weight
of
beads
without
drug
× 100
(3)
Encapsulation
Efficiency
(
EE
%
)
=
Actually
drug
loaded
in
beads
Theoratical
drug
loaded
× 100

The release of DOX from the carrier was measured at 37 °C in the dark over a 72 h. At predetermined time intervals, 2 mL of the buffer solution was sampled and replaced with an equal volume of fresh buffer. The DOX-loaded carrier (10 mg) was immersed in buffer solutions with pH values of 1.2 (for 3 h), 6.8 (for 4 h), and 7.4 (for the remaining duration) to simulate the three stages of the gastrointestinal tract: gastric, small intestinal, and colonic fluids [26]. The concentration of released DOX was determined using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer at a maximum absorption wavelength (λ max) of 483 nm. Calibration curves were constructed by plotting the absorbance against concentration, as shown in Eq. (4).
(4)
Cumulative
release =
The
amount
of
released
DOX
The
amount
of
loaded
DOX
× 100

ce:section.ce:section-title: Kinetic studies of in-vitro drug release
ce:section.ce:para: DOX release kinetics from the synthesized carrier was evaluated by using the Weibull, Higuchi, zero-order, Peppas, and first-order models [47].

The antibacterial activity of GM-SA/Fe3O4@CuO@CQD was tested against Gram-positive Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) and Gram-negative Escherichia coli (E. coli) by colony-forming unit (CFU) counting. The GM-SA/Fe3O4@CuO@CQD, CuO NPs, and OPE were dispersed in sterile water via ultrasonication. One milliliter of bacterial suspension at CFU/mL was mixed with 1 mL of each sample. After incubation at 37 °C for 2 h, 100 μL of the mixture was serially diluted in PBS and plated on nutrient agar. The plates were incubated at 37 °C for 24 h, and viable colonies were counted. The control group consisted of bacterial suspension without synthesized samples. All materials and plates (except membranes) were sterilized by autoclaving and UV light before testing.

The antioxidant activity of the carrier was evaluated using the DPPH (1,1-diphenyl-2-picrylhydrazyl) radical scavenging assay, which measures the ability of antioxidants to reduce DPPH [49]. A 0.2 M DPPH solution was prepared in 96 % ethanol, and carrier at concentrations 2.5, 5, 10, 20, and 40 mg/L was added to 3 mL of DPPH solution. The mixture was shaken and left in the dark at room temperature for 3 h. Absorbance reduction was measured at 517 nm using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer, with ethanol as a blank. A control sample, consisting of 3 mL DPPH solution without the carrier, was also prepared. The scavenging activity was calculated using Eq. (5):
(5)
I n h i b i t i o n
% =
A B
−
A A
A B
× 100
Where AB and AA are the absorbance of the control and test carrier, respectively.

The cytotoxicity and biocompatibility of GM-SA/Fe3O4@CuO@CQD and GM-SA/Fe3O4@CuO@CQD-DOX against HT-29 cells were assessed using MTT assay. HT-29 cells were cultured in DMEM containing 10 % fetal bovine serum (FBS) and 1 % penicillin-streptomycin. Cells were seeded in 96-well plates and incubated with the samples at 37 °C in a 5 % CO2 atmosphere for 48 h. After incubation, an MTT solution was added, and cells were incubated for an additional 4 h. DMSO was then added to dissolve the formazan crystals, and absorbance was measured at 490 nm by using an ELISA reader. Cell viability was calculated by using Eq. (6) [50,51].
(6)
Cell
viability
( % )
=
OD treated
OD control
× 100
OD treated is the absorbance of cells treated with samples, and OD control is the absorbance of the control group (untreated cells + culture medium + DMSO).

FTIR and XRD analyses were used to characterize the chemical composition and crystalline structure of GM-SA/Fe3O4@CuO@CQD and its components (Fig. 1 a and b). Fig. 1a displays the FTIR spectra of Fe3O4, CuO, CQD, GM, SA, and the final bio-nanocomposite. In the FTIR spectrum of Fe3O4 MNPs, the peaks observed at 563 and 3426 cm−1 correspond to Fe–O and O–H bonds, respectively [52,53]. The peaks at 1091, 2925, and 3433 cm−1 in GM spectra revealed C–O, C–H aliphatic, and OH bonds, respectively confirming its carbohydrate polymeric structure. Also, the absorbance bands at 804 and 870 cm−1 correspond to glycosidic linkages that confirmed the presence of β-d-mannopyranose and α-d-galactopyranose in structure [45,54]. Moreover, in the spectra of SA, OPE, CuO, and CQD the peaks at about 1100, 1385, 1625, 2900, and 3400 cm−1 are associated with C–O, –COO, carbonyl group (CO), C–H aliphatic and O–H bonds respectively. Remarkably, the broad peak at about 1600 cm−1 is due to the overlap of CO groups with CC bonds in CQD structure. The peak at 600 cm−1 in the CuO spectra indicates the presence of Cu–O bond in the structure [42,43,55]. Upon combining these components to form the GM-SA/Fe3O4@CuO@CQD carrier, characteristic peaks from individual materials remain visible, though slight shifts and intensity reductions indicate successful integration and intermolecular interactions within the composite structure.

The XRD pattern of Fe3O4 MNPs exhibits intense peaks at 2θ = 30.14°, 35.62°, 43.36°, 53.82°, 57.34°, and 62.88°, which correspond with (220), (311), (400), (422), (511), and (440) planes, respectively, confirming its crystalline structure [56]. Additionally, GM pattern displays sharp peaks at about 2θ = 32° and 46°, which show high crystalline structure due to the regular arrangement of galactose and mannose units in its structure [45,57]. Three diffraction peaks located at 2θ values of 13.99° (110), 21.33° (200), and 39.44° (amorphous halo) are due to SA [58]. XRD analysis bared small distinct diffraction peaks at 22.81°, 35.28°, 46.8°, and 67.18°, that indexed the planes 110, 111, 022, and 311 of the face-centered-cubic structure of CuO NPs based on previous studies [59,60]. The d-spacing values of CQDs at 2θ = 24.68°, 28.39°, 31.53°, and 40.54° which is proportional to the (20 0), (1 1 0), (1 0 0) and (1 0 1) planes of graphitic carbon respectively, revealing a polycrystalline nature [61]. Overall, due to the GM-SA/Fe3O4@CuO@CQD spectra, the observant peaks indicated excellent structural integration and confirmed the successful synthesizing of the carrier.

SEM and EDX/MAP analyses were conducted to examine the morphology and elemental composition of the GM-SA/Fe3O4@CuO@CQD. The Fe3O4 MNPs and CQDs displayed well-defined spherical particles with average sizes of approximately 40 nm and 14 nm respectively, showing uniform distribution as seen in Fig. 2 a and b. Fig. 2c shows that the CuO NPs stabilized with OPE were nearly spherical, with an average size of about 40 nm, indicating successful synthesis and effective stabilization. In the SEM images, the drug-free carrier (Fig. 2d) exhibits a smooth and homogeneous surface. In contrast, the DOX-loaded carrier (Fig. 2f) shows a rough and uneven surface, suggesting successful drug incorporation into the composite matrix, leading to structural changes [62]. Furthermore, EDX analysis confirmed the presence of elements including Fe, Cu, C, N, Ca, and O in the components. Additionally, the mapping analysis (MAP) showed a uniform and consistent distribution of elements on the surface of the carriers, indicating successful integration of the NPs.

The magnetic properties of Fe3O4 MNPs differ significantly in comparison to MNPs embedded in the bio-nanocomposites hydrogel. Fe3O4 MNPs exhibit superparamagnetic behavior due to their nanoscale dimensions, which allows them to rapidly magnetize under an external magnetic field [63,64]. The Fe3O4 MNPs magnetization saturation (Ms) is 59.44 emu g−1 while, when Fe3O4 MNPs are incorporated into a hydrogel matrix, surface interactions and environmental constraints within the hydrogel reduce the saturation magnetization to 4.23 emu g−1. These alterations are likely due to spatial constraints, enhanced dipole-dipole interactions among NPs, and also the presence of non-magnetic material on the surface of magnetic particles (Fig. 3 a) [65].

In Fig. 3b, the optical characteristics of CQDs derived from tea leaves in an aqueous solution are depicted. The UV/Vis spectra of the CQDs solution exhibit two prominent absorption peaks at 276 nm and 330 nm. These peaks are associated with the π−π∗ transition within the aromatic sp2-hybridized domains and the n−π∗ transition within the sp3-hybridized domains, respectively [66]. The fluorescence spectra of the aqueous CQDs display a strong blue emission at 400 nm, confirming that they possess a significant Stokes shift [66]. When CQDs are incorporated into a bio-nanocomposite hydrogel, their emission peak shifts to 430 nm. This shift reflects modifications in the band structure and electronic environment of CQDs, arising from interactions with the bio-nanocomposites hydrogel. This integration results in an increase in dot size, alterations in the dielectric environment, and the creation of new sites for electron excitation, ultimately changing the optical properties of CQDs [67].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Swelling and DOX release behavior studies
ce:section.ce:para: The investigation of water-hydrogel interactions is critically important for drug delivery applications. Determining these parameters is crucial for predicting drug loading capacity, release behavior, nutrient permeation, and the transport of other relevant molecules [68]. Hydrogels with crosslinked polymer networks exhibit sensitivity to environmental factors such as pH, resulting in changes to their physical properties, including swelling and deswelling. Fig. 4 a shows the swelling behavior of the carrier over 72 h in three different buffer solutions (pH 1.2, 6.8, and 7.4), which mimic gastric fluid, small intestine fluid, and colonic fluid, respectively. At pH 1.2, the swelling ratio (SR) showed a slight consistent increase, demonstrating the carrier in the acidic conditions of the stomach. Conversely, at pH 7.4, a significant and continuous increase in SR was observed, reaching up to 1554 %. After 72 h at pH 7.4, the carrier exhibited mass loss, leading to a decrease in SR. This suggests the gradual disintegration of the polymer network and the breakdown of the carrier structure under these conditions. These findings indicate that the responsiveness of carriers to pH can significantly influence the effectiveness and stability of controlled DDSs (see Table 1).

The DOX encapsulation and loading efficiency of GM-SA/Fe3O4@CuO@CQD was 97 %, indicating its high effectiveness. The satisfactory DOX loading capacity is attributed to the abundance of functional groups within the bio-nanocomposite's structure. The polymer's functional groups, including OH and COOH, enhance the interaction with DOX, leading to its encapsulation via hydrogen bonding, van der Waals forces, π-π stacking, and electrostatic interactions [69]. Under drug loading conditions (pH 7.4), the polymer's carboxylic acid groups deprotonate and acquire a negative charge (COO−), facilitating the absorption and encapsulation of the positively charged DOX. Additionally, the Fe3O4 MNPs, possessing a negative surface charge, enhance DOX loading through electrostatic attraction with the drug's positive charge. The π-π interactions between the aromatic rings of the quantum dots and DOX, along with van der Waals forces between the hydrophobic regions of the polymer and the drug, are believed to play a crucial role in the effective loading of DOX [70]. Drug release was assessed in different pH (1.2, 6.8, and 7.4) at 37 °C over 72 h. These pH conditions simulate human stomach environments, small intestine, and large intestine. As depicted in Fig. 4b, only 13.85 % of DOX was released in an acidic environment (pH 1.2) after 72 h, a significantly lower amount compared to the release at pH 7.4. A high amount of DOX (86.77 %) was released at pH 7.4, highlighting the pH sensitivity of the drug release mechanism. The findings suggested that the engineered carrier is a promising option for anti-cancer DDSs. Also, kinetic parameters are presented in Table 2
. Due to the obtained results, the Peppas model is the best fit for DOX release profile.

The antibacterial activity of OPE, CuO NPs established and reduced by OPE, and GM/SA/Fe3O4@CuO@CQD was evaluated against Staphylococcus aureus (Gram-positive) and Escherichia coli (Gram-negative). Bacterial populations within the tumor microenvironment contribute to CRC progression by inducing chronic inflammation, inhibiting apoptosis, and activating oncogenic signaling pathways. Therefore, modulating these microbial interactions through targeted antibacterial activity can be a strategic approach in CRC therapy [71]. The antibacterial properties of OPE can be attributed to bioactive compounds such as tannins, saponins, phenolics, essential oils, and flavonoids. These compounds interact with cellular proteins, leading to the disruption of bacterial cell membranes and inhibition of bacterial growth (Fig. 5 a) [72]. CuO-based materials exhibit multiple antibacterial mechanisms, which can operate independently or synergistically to enhance antibacterial effects. These mechanisms are classified into three primary modes of action: (1) cellular uptake, (2) ion release, and (3) generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS). These factors collectively contribute to damage of the cell membrane, metabolic inhibition, lipid peroxidation, protein oxidation, DNA degradation, nucleic acid dysfunction, and intracellular leakage. Notably, copper and CuO-based nanomaterials generate ROS both in solution and within cells after uptake. CuO-based materials produce significant quantities of ROS, including hydroxyl radicals (·OH), superoxide anions (·O2−), and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) [73]. The antibacterial activity of the synthesized carrier was observed against both Gram-positive (99.34 ± 0.06 %) and Gram-negative (94.36 ± 0.08 %) bacteria, with higher effectiveness against Gram-positive strains. The increased resistance of Gram-negative bacteria is likely due to the additional outer membrane composed primarily of densely packed lipopolysaccharide molecules, which form a barrier that limits NP penetration and restricts the entry of metal ions into bacterial cells [74]. The enhanced antibacterial activity of the carrier compared to OPE highlights the efficacy of incorporating Cu NPs into the bio-nanocomposites. The superior antibacterial performance of GM/SA/Fe3O4@CuO@CQD underscores their potential for biomedical applications.

The antioxidant potential of the carrier was evaluated using the DPPH assay (IC50 = 2.1 mg/mL), which is particularly relevant for drug carriers in CRC therapy, as oxidative stress plays a crucial role in tumor progression and therapeutic resistance. The results demonstrate significant antioxidant properties of the carrier, as evidenced by the color change of the DPPH solution from dark violet to light yellow, indicating their ability to neutralize free radicals (Fig. 5b). The incorporation of CuO NPs into the carrier significantly enhances their antioxidant activity due to the hydroxyl groups on the CuO surface, which facilitate electron transfer. This electron transfer leads to a reduction in absorbance at 517 nm, confirming effective DPPH radical scavenging [75]. The synthesizing of NPs by OPE further contributes to the antioxidant capacity of the carrier, because a natural reducing agent that enriches the nanoparticles with bioactive compounds such as flavonoids and phenolics [76]. In conclusion, the combination of polysaccharides, metal NPs, and bioactive compounds enhances the antioxidant activity of the carrier, offering the potential for reducing oxidative stress in cancer treatment and improving targeted drug delivery in CRC therapy.

The MTT assay, a widely used colorimetric technique, evaluates in vitro cytotoxicity by measuring cell viability based on metabolic activity [77]. This study evaluates the cytotoxicity, biocompatibility, and pharmacological activity of free DOX, GM-SA/Fe3O4@CuO@CQD, and GM-SA/Fe3O4@CuO@CQD-DOX against HT-29 cells (Fig. 6 ). The results demonstrated that the IC50 value for free DOX was 64 μg/mL, whereas the DOX-loaded carrier was 128 μg/mL, indicating reduced cytotoxicity due to the controlled drug release from the bio-nanocomposite. This increase in IC50 suggests a slower, sustained release of the drug, allowing for targeted delivery to cancer cells, thereby reducing systemic toxicity and minimizing side effects. Moreover, the incorporation of CuO NPs into the carrier system not only enhances drug delivery but also exerts direct anticancer effects by generating ROS that induce oxidative stress and damage cellular components. These NPs contribute to cellular damage, DNA strand breaks, and apoptosis induction in cancer cells, significantly enhancing the therapeutic efficacy of the treatment [78,79]. Consequently, the carrier containing DOX not only enables controlled and sustained drug release but also improves the overall anticancer activity compared to free DOX. This system showed prolonged drug retention at the tumor site and enhanced the effectiveness of CRC treatment in the HT-29 cell line while minimizing systemic side effects.

This study introduces a pH-responsive magnetic bio-nanocomposite hydrogel as a promising carrier for targeted CRC drug delivery. The hydrogel, composed of GM and SA, demonstrated improved structural stability and drug-loading efficiency. The incorporation of Fe3O4 MNPs improved the hydrogel's magnetite properties, while the modification with CQDs and CuO NPs, synthesized via a green approach, imparted notable antioxidant, antibacterial, and anticancer functionalities. Comprehensive physicochemical characterizations (FT-IR, SEM, EDX, VSM, and XRD) confirmed the effective integration of these components, highlighting their synergistic effects in enhancing hydrogel performance. In vitro drug release studies under simulated gastrointestinal conditions demonstrated that the GM-SA/Fe3O4@CuO@CQD hydrogel exhibited pH-sensitive swelling and controlled drug release, ensuring sustained DOX delivery at physiological pH (7.4). Additionally, cytotoxicity assays against HT-29 CRC cells revealed that DOX-loaded hydrogels effectively induced cancer cell apoptosis while maintaining biocompatibility. These findings underscore the potential of this smart hydrogel system as an efficient and biocompatible drug delivery platform for CRC therapy. By integrating targeted drug release, magnetic responsiveness, and antimicrobial properties, this bio-nanocomposite presents a multifunctional approach for enhanced therapeutic efficacy and reduced systemic side effects. Future studies focusing on in vivo evaluations and clinical translation will further validate its applicability in advanced CRC treatment strategies.

Shabnam Tahmasebi: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Software, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Reza Mohammadi: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Resources.
"This research did not receive any specific grant from funding agencies in the public, commercial, or not-for-profit sectors."

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the valuable technical support and assistance provided by colleagues at the University of Tabriz.

ce:section.ce:para:

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jsamd.2025.100892.
